reasoning_summary: For live run `20260421_175700_bf16_gemm_v1_05086a14` at `26.385408 ms`, the regular 128x128 sibling proved that lower register pressure alone is not enough: registers dropped from 202 to 168 and occupancy moved from the 2-CTA class to 3 CTAs/SM, but barrier rose from 6.39% to 10.15%, short-scoreboard rose from 1.86% to 6.74%, and end-to-end runtime regressed by 1.578 ms versus the accepted PTX base `20260421_172601_bf16_gemm_v1_117cd3e7` at `24.806945 ms`. That falsifies promoting the regular sibling as the new default surface. The next round should therefore restore the accepted compact PTX consume path and import only one lever at a time. I rank a PTX-only launch-bounds retune first because the current run shows that 3-CTA residency can be valuable if it is separated from the regular sibling's barrier tax. I keep a surgical PTX barrier-trim second because synchronization is now the clearest regressed signal, and I leave grouped_rows=2 third as the cheaper opposite-direction launch-order probe after the prior grouped_rows=8 loss.

family_audit.rationale: The regular sibling proved that lower registers and higher occupancy are helpful local signals, but by themselves they did not produce a faster end-to-end kernel. Future moves should preserve the accepted compact PTX consume path while selectively importing the register-budget win.

Barrier is now the clearest regressed signal at 10.15%, so a surgical wait/sync trim on the PTX surface is back in scope. Full x32 staged-family promotion is still rejected because that family already produced a measured loss.
The 256x128 / 64x64-warp family remains the structural ceiling, but this run changed too many local levers at once. The next move should first isolate the accepted PTX base with one narrower edit.

family_audit.family: Coalescing Access / Data Reuse / L2 Cache
family_audit.rationale: The profile is still not bandwidth-bound. DRAM is only 14.06% of peak and the dominant regression is synchronization rather than raw memory throughput.
The structured run does not surface bank-conflict pressure as the dominant limiter on this regression.

directions.name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Retest Three-CTA Residency There
directions.hypothesis: The regular sibling regression shows that 3-CTA residency is not bad by itself; the bad part is the regular sibling's barrier-heavy consume path. Restoring the accepted compact PTX hot-band launch and then tightening only the PTX kernel launch-bounds target from 2 to 3 CTAs/SM is the cleanest way to test whether the occupancy win can be kept without inheriting the 10.15% barrier tax.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The accepted compact PTX surface is still register-limited, but the beneficial part of the current regression may be the 3-CTA residency rather than the sibling kernel body.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is only a two-part local edit on the accepted PTX surface, but a tighter launch-bounds target can still introduce spills or weaken instruction scheduling.
ranking_notes: This is the best isolation experiment because it keeps the accepted compact PTX surface and imports only the one positive signal from the regressed run: higher CTA residency.
It directly matches the latest delta table: occupancy improved, but barrier and short-scoreboard regressed. This edit is designed to keep the first while avoiding the latter.
It is lower risk than another full sibling swap or a fresh tiling reopen.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again without a clear barrier reduction, or if build / NCU evidence suggests spill-driven residency forcing.
expected_local_changes: Restore the default fixed-shape hot-band dispatch to the compact PTX microkernel surface.
Retune the PTX hot-band launch-bounds annotation from the current 2-CTA target toward 3-CTA residency.
Leave grouped rows, shared-memory footprint, and stage depth unchanged in the first validation pass.

directions.name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Trim Barrier Cadence Without Shared-Memory Blowup
directions.hypothesis: The regular sibling regression moved the bottleneck from long-scoreboard to barrier plus short-scoreboard. A surgical edit on the accepted compact PTX surface that trims wait/sync cadence without adding the extra x32 shared-memory staging buffers could recover the barrier loss while preserving the compact row-pair microkernel's better long-scoreboard and mio-throttle behavior.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Single-K barrier cadence and CTA handoff overhead on the accepted compact PTX hot-band surface remain the unresolved latency tax once the row-pair live-range reset is in place.
directions.risk: High. This is still PTX-microkernel surgery, and it can easily break correctness or recreate the staged-family regressions if the shared-memory footprint or synchronization contract grows.
ranking_notes: This is the strongest barrier-focused fallback once the regular sibling has been ruled out as a net win.
It stays below dir_01 because it is much riskier and touches the PTX microkernel loop directly.
It is still more aligned with the latest live bottleneck than a fresh tiling reopen.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory rises above the accepted 22,016 B footprint or if barrier does not improve materially against the accepted PTX base.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted compact PTX microkernel launch surface before editing the wait/sync cadence.
Trim the single-K consumer barrier / wait-group handoff without allocating a second full staged buffer pair.
Keep grouped rows and hot-band geometry unchanged while testing the synchronization change.

directions.name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Try Grouped Rows Equals Two
directions.hypothesis: The earlier grouped_rows=8 test already showed that deeper B reuse was the wrong launch-order direction. After the current run demonstrated that occupancy alone is insufficient, the cheapest remaining launch-order probe is to restore the compact PTX base and reduce kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows from 4 to 2, favoring tighter A locality and shorter grouped traversal without changing stage depth or the consume kernel body.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The accepted PTX grouped-row traversal may still be mismatched to the A/B locality balance on the fixed hot-band surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. This is a very small change, but the upside may be noise-sized because the opposite grouped-row direction already failed and launch order was never the main bottleneck.
ranking_notes: This stays in the set as the cheapest opposite-direction launch-order test after the earlier grouped_rows=8 loss.
It ranks below dir_02 because the current live regression is more clearly about barrier cadence than grouped traversal.
Use it only after the PTX launch-bounds isolation and barrier-trim probes fail or plateau.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if runtime change is within noise or if barrier / scoreboard behavior does not improve after one measurement.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted compact PTX hot-band dispatch if needed.
Leave launch-bounds, stage depth, and the hot-band consume helper unchanged for the first measurement.

notes: Round 2 treats the regular 128x128 sibling as a measured_loss for accepted-base promotion: it improved occupancy and long-scoreboard locally, but the barrier / short-scoreboard regression dominated total runtime. The next moves all restore the compact PTX surface first and then change one lever at a time.
